speaker: interviewee
text: No. You know, I am thank with the writer of my book, Lutz van Dijk, a very good connection. He helped me a lot for all documents, even my documents of the prison. And he make the-- he made the recession. In Poland, in Austria, in Germany, he couldn't find anything. Probably, it is true, as I thought. I was the reason of his death. It means, he was killed, he was shotted, because for Germany, with its high people, it was absolutely unbelieve that somebody will have contact, sexual contact, with a Polish man-- with Polish boy. No we-- but, as I told you, I have all documentation just now-- not maybe exactly, like-- but for the prison time from this and where we was, I have. This year, I received from the Red Cross also documentation that I was banished for five years. And there are some so stupid sentences. Let's say for the nationality, they written 'unknown'. I was beaten, I was arrested as a Polish. They called, you Polish pig as a-- and they write that this is 'unknown nation'. When I received this document, I was so angry. As I told you before, I had a very good contact with the German organization, also with Magnus-- Magnus Hirschfeld. He fighted. He was a Jew who's sex-- a homosexual-- he fighted for us, for our rules, for our rights. And this magazine had the name-- his name-- Magnus. I receive always free of charge. And many times I get some information about me, about my life in Warsaw, to this magazine, to all homosexuals in Germany. Because they wanted to know what's happen me, how is me with life after this operation, after this. They wanted to know. And but when I received this part, this year, after waiting four years for the one part, and it's stupid, but I couldn't-- I couldn't keep this. So I wrote to Magnus this letter and-- as following-- [GERMAN] I wrote on 2 February, 1995. Here is Stefan K. This is my address. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: As you know, I have all documentation. I will give maybe the documents from the Germany letters also. On the begin, when I wrote-- yes. First the letter-- you can here see. You can make the-- or maybe you later will make the copy?

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut. Stefan, can you describe this picture for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's-- I was maybe about 10-years-old. It was in my born city, Torun-- the center-- [NON-ENGLISH] Street, near of the Rathaus, municipal. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That's me. I was at this picture 16-years-old. It was made in June, 1941. I was with some boys at the Vistula. We picked, and I fried the-- some fish we find-- no-- we got from the River Vistula-- my last holiday spent with some friends before they arrested. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is me in 1942. It was made in the wardrobe of theater. I remember this picture make me the [NON-ENGLISH]-- one of the gay boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- you're wearing a dress. What dress is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was for gypsy suit-- so big hat in a circle. And we had to read some [NON-ENGLISH] like this. It was not nice. I preferred the costume in the second operetta in [NON-ENGLISH]-- more nicer. I was in little shorts, and so. But this costume was not nice. But we had to wear it. Then for the-- it was on the-- no. It was for the set spectacular when they had those-- [NON-ENGLISH SINGING] That was this. But at the beginning, we had differently-- white. This wasn't nice. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's me in 1947 when I was working as a waiter by American mess. It was in Dachau. There were also war crime stages for the-- in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was working there. It was nice time. As you see, I'm well. But I would have never landed this job. But they agree that I spoke a little English. And I could do my job as a waiter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how old you are?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this time, I was 21-- 21, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's me, also in 1946, also in Dachau during my short time working. I met a Polish soldier. He was working for American. There were many. They were as a guards, only, therefore, they had the American uniform, but specially changed in differently color. They had a blue color.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That's me in 1947, near-- of this house, where I was living in Munich. And you see there, I am still well dressed. At this time, I was working as a waiter by American. And my PX ration were changed, or compensate made for the-- with some German tailors, or this, and before. He tailored me a nice suit. And I wanted-- especially this picture I wanted to send to my mother when I received her contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she receive the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, yeah, because I have from my mother. I sent, and then I received. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That's me after the war in Poland, in Warsaw, approximately-- maybe 1960, when I finished my study. I think so. But this-- exactly, I-- I cannot remember. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's me when I was 14-years-old. That was this uniform. I was dressed for the gymnasium, but I never went to gymnasium. According to this reason, the war began on 1 September, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the picture also taken in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was made before the war. That was made in Poland, yes. And I had original, but I sent to my friend, writer, Lutz van Dijk. And he-- he wanted to make the copy for the next book, maybe after my death. And he sent me all pictures to Poland. Unfortunately, by logistic-- unfortunately, I have never received this letter. I don't know who did. And then I ask him to give me maybe some, because if I want to see the-- and he sent me them in this way, in this-- this-- by fax, or like-- the copy. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a very good document. I received this thanks-- the writer, Lutz van Dijk. He received from the German prison-- from the-- of the-- from the prison. And there is exactly written the time how long I spent in the prison. This is the date mentioned there from 3 December, 1942 to 15, 5th, 1945. It means only to-- they gave only to the time when I escaped. But before the time, by Gestapo, they didn't measure. They are writing from 7 December. But anyhow, it's the document. That is the few months before. They didn't mention it here. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a very important document. This document thanks the writer, Lutz van Dijk. I received from the Red Cross in [NON-ENGLISH]. They have probably all the documents from all displaced persons. But this is only one part of my documents. And there is written my name, where I was born, my father and my family, my nationality. But stupidly, I write there 'unknown nationality.' I am Polish. They took me as a Polish, and there was written as an unknown. And on the other side of this here is written exactly how long I was punished-- for how long-- for five years. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the second side of this document. And there is written that I was punished for 5 years from 2 December, 1942 to the 2 December, 1947. It's mean if I didn't escape, I had to be the prison to the end of my punishment. It's mean until 2 December, 1947, because in Germany, the paragraph 175 was valid even until 1968. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This is my displaced card. It is the document that I was a displaced person on this and this number. I received this card in Munich by the international camp for all refugees. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the second side of my card. And there is written that this is like a passport. This was my card. This was the document. I could go everywhere. And I was treated much better than Germans, because I was a displaced person. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the document from 1946, my first job after my prison time when I worked by American as a kitchen help. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This is the document when I was working by American mess-- officer's mess in Dachau. Because before, I worked only as a kitchen help. But then, when I learned a little English, I could work also by American officers. [PAUSES FOR 28 SECONDS] This is my last document of the working by American officer's mess in Dachau, before I went to Poland. There is also mentioned that any help will be recommended to me in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in June, 1947?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In June, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The last work. And then, few days later, I-- I left Poland-- I left Germany to Poland to my mother. [PAUSES FOR 24 SECONDS] As you know, I wrote many letters to the German government concerning my indemnification. And this is the last letter. And they write they cannot do-- they cannot give me any indemnification. And they-- they stay this decision, which was given in the previous letter. It's mean that the Polish government has received their money, and they cannot give me nothing. This is the last letter, so I never wrote more to German government concerning indemnification. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So there are two editions of my book, The first one, smaller, is German. It was published approximately four years ago. And the bigger one is in English. It has been published this year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Frankly speaking, I prefer the English one. The translation is excellent. Even the edition, it's bigger, nicer, and the paper-- everything is OK. I had the opportunity to talk to a lady, [NON-ENGLISH] in-- I don't know where-- by phone from New York, from the publisher. And I gave her my congratulation. She was wondering that I speak English, because first, she wanted to speak with me German. But her German is rather very bad. She writes and reads. She reads, but she cannot speak. She had not practice. But I hope to meet her during my traveling in the United States.
